title: Ensure 'PHP version' is currently supported (if in use)

input_row.title: Ensure 'PHP version' is currently supported (if in use)
input_row.description: Periodically, older versions of PHP may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of PHP for App Service deployment slots is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
input_row.rationale: Deprecated and unsupported versions of programming and scripting languages can present vulnerabilities which may not be addressed or may not be addressable.
input_row.audit: Take note of the currently supported versions of PHP here: https://www.php.net/supported-versions.php Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, ensure that for a Stack of PHP, the Major version and Minor version reflect a currently supported release. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list apps: az webapp list For each app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az webapp deployment slot list --resource-group --name For each deployment slot, run the following command to get the PHP version: az resource show --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --query properties.[linuxFxVersion,phpVersion] If PHP is in use, ensure the version is currently supported. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: f466b2a6-823d-470d-8ea5-b031e72d79ae - Name: 'App Service app slots that use PHP should use a specified 'PHP version''
input_row.remediation: Note: No action is required if PHP is not in use. Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, for a Stack of PHP, set the Major version and Minor version to a currently supported release. 7. Click Save. 8. Click Continue. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI Run the following command to list supported runtimes: az webapp list-runtimes For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command with the appropriate parameters to update the PHP version: az resource update --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --set properties.[linuxFxVersion|phpVersion]=""
input_row.impact: If your app is written using version-dependent features or libraries, they may not be available on more recent versions. If you wish to update, research the impact thoroughly.
input_row.default_value: The version is selected during creation.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access the AWS Management Console**: 
   - Navigate to the AWS Management Console and select the "Elastic Beanstalk" service if the application is hosted there, or "Lambda" if using AWS Lambda.

2. **Find the Application**: 
   - In Elastic Beanstalk, look for the environment associated with your application.
   - For Lambda, navigate to the "Functions" page and find the function you're interested in.

3. **Check for PHP Usage**: 
   - In Elastic Beanstalk, check if the environment is set to run PHP. Look at the platform version details.
   - For Lambda, check the runtime of the function and see if it is set to a PHP-compatible runtime.

4. **Identify the PHP Version**: 
   - For Elastic Beanstalk, note the platform version as it typically indicates the PHP version being used. Check the [AWS Elastic Beanstalk Platforms](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk/latest/dg/what-is-eb.html) documentation for PHP version correlations.
   - For Lambda, review the runtime used in the function configuration to identify the PHP version.

5. **Verify Version Support**: 
   - Cross-reference the identified PHP version with the official supported versions available here: [PHP Supported Versions](https://www.php.net/supported-versions.php).

6. **Determine Compliance**: 
   - If the running version of PHP is deprecated or not on the supported list, the application is non-compliant.

7. **Document Findings**: 
   - Record the current PHP version and its compliance status (compliant or non-compliant).
   - If non-compliant, detail the proposed corrective actions based on the remediation steps outlined in your organization’s internal policies or the AWS Elastic Beanstalk documentation.

8. **Review Other Applications**: 
   - Repeat steps 2-7 for each Elastic Beanstalk environment or Lambda function that may be utilizing PHP.

**Expected secure configuration**: The PHP version in use is one that is currently supported according to [PHP Supported Versions](https://www.php.net/supported-versions.php).
